slides.title: Stop Rewriting the Same Code
slides.body: **Inheritance** lets one class borrow everything from another. Write shared code once in a *parent class*, and every child class gets it for free.
slides.tip: The magic phrase: if a Dog **is-a** Animal, Dog should inherit from Animal.

slides.title: Build the Parent Class First
slides.body: The parent holds everything all animals share — a `name`, an `eat()` method, a `speak()` method. Child classes start from this blueprint.

slides.title: Inherit with One Word
slides.body: Put the parent name in parentheses: `class Dog(Animal)`. That single line gives `Dog` *all* of `Animal`'s methods — no copy-pasting needed.
slides.code:
class Dog(Animal):
    pass  # inherits everything!

fido = Dog("Fido")
fido.eat()
slides.tip: Even with just `pass`, Dog already works because it inherits from Animal.

slides.title: Override to Customise Behaviour
slides.body: Define a method with the **same name** in the child class and Python uses the child's version. Dogs bark; cats meow — each gets its own `speak()`.

slides.title: Use super() to Call the Parent
slides.body: When the child has its own `__init__`, call `super().__init__(...)` first so the parent can do **its** setup. Skipping it means `self.name` never gets set.
slides.tip: Always make super().__init__() the FIRST line of the child's __init__.

slides.title: You've Got Inheritance!
slides.body: One parent, many children — shared code lives in one place, unique behaviour lives in each subclass. **Write less, reuse more, break fewer things.**
slides.tip: Next level: use isinstance(fido, Animal) to check the "is-a" relationship at runtime.
